[JS Weird World] No.12 How Async Callbacks Actually Work
JsWeirdWorld
JavaScript 全攻略:克服JS奇怪的部分

A few chapters back I talked about how JavaScript runs synchronously, one line at a time. You've probably also heard the word "async" thrown around a lot. But what does async actually mean? In this post we look at the event queue and how JavaScript handles synchronous and asynchronous work without ever really breaking its one-line-at-a-time rule.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/jsweirdworld-11-scope-es6-let/
[JS Weird World] No.11 Scope, ES6, and let
JavaScript Full Course: Conquering the Weird Parts

Scope is basically the area where a variable can be accessed. In this part we look at ES6's new keyword let — how it works like var, why it didn't replace var, and the block scope idea it brings to the table.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/js-weird-world-10-scope-chain/
[JS Weird World] No.10 The Scope Chain
JavaScript: Conquering the Weird Parts

Next up is the scope chain. Honestly this one isn't too hard, because we've already practiced it a bunch in the earlier posts — and the last chapter basically already touched on the scope chain. Like always, we'll learn it straight from the course example code.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/js-weird-world-09-variable-environments/
[JS Weird World] No.9 Functions, Execution Context, and the Variable Environment
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts of JS

The variable environment is really just about where you create your variables. Basically, all you need to care about is "where" a variable was created. In this post we walk through a small example that shows how the same variable name can live in three different places without stepping on each other, and how return lets you carry a result back to global scope.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/15/js-weird-world-08-function-invocation-execution-stack/
[JS Weird World] No.8 Function Invocation and the Execution Stack

Invocation just means calling a function. It's a really important concept, so when someone says "function invocation" or "invocation function," they just mean running that function — and we run it with the parentheses (). This post walks through what actually happens when you call a function, how a new execution context gets created each time, and how that builds up the execution stack.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/15/jsweirdworld-07-single-threaded-synchronous/
[JS Weird World] No.7 Quick Concept Check: Single-Threaded and Synchronous Execution

"Single-threaded" sounds like a fancy, complicated term, but it really just means JavaScript runs one instruction at a time — it can't run two at once. Once you get that, the difference between single-threaded and multi-threaded clicks, and you'll even spot the same word on the hardware side. This post is a quick reminder of two concepts: single-threaded and synchronous execution.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/15/js-weird-world-06-code-execution/
[JS Weird World] No.6 Execution Context: Running Your Code

When JavaScript runs your code, it actually goes through two phases — a creation phase and an execution phase. In this post I break down what happens in each one, using a small example to show how variables get set up first and then assigned line by line.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/15/js-weird-world-05-undefined-vs-not-defined/
[JS Weird World] No.5 A Quick Reminder: JavaScript and Undefined
JavaScript Mastery: Conquering the Weird Parts

We've touched on undefined a little already, but now it's time to go deeper into the relationship between JavaScript and undefined. This post digs into the difference between undefined and "not defined", why typeof returns undefined, and the one thing you should never do — manually assigning undefined to a variable.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/15/js-weird-world-04-creation-and-hoisting/
[JS Weird World] No.4 The Execution Context: Creation and Hoisting

This chapter digs into one of the genuinely strange corners of JavaScript: "creation and hoisting." It's a topic that trips up a ton of people. We'll walk through what actually happens when JavaScript runs your code, why a variable can come back as undefined before you ever assigned it, and what hoisting really means (hint: your code doesn't actually move).
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/15/js-weird-world-03-global-environment-object/
[JS Weird World] No.3 The Global Environment and the Global Object

The "global environment" is tightly tied to the idea of an execution context. When your program first runs, it runs inside the global environment — a prebuilt wrapper someone else wrote, a "Global" that holds both the code you're about to run and the code that's already running. This post walks through the global object, the special `this` variable, and where your variables and functions actually live.
